chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing acute septic arthritis, including antibiotic duration, pathogen-specific therapy, and assessment for adjacent osteomyelitis, with emphasis on imaging and clinical markers.
learning_objectives: Understand empiric antibiotic choices for acute septic arthritis based on age and pathogen likelihood.
Recognize when to extend antibiotic duration, particularly in cases of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus or adjacent osteomyelitis.
Identify clinical and imaging markers for adjacent osteomyelitis and their impact on treatment strategy.
key_facts: Empiric antibiotics should cover Staphylococcus aureus and age-appropriate organisms, with cloxacillin or cefazolin for methicillin-susceptible cases and vancomycin for methicillin-resistant cases.
Uncomplicated septic arthritis typically requires 2-3 weeks of antibiotics, extended to 4 weeks for methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus or adjacent osteomyelitis.
Risk factors for adjacent osteomyelitis include hip infection, delayed treatment, severe Staphylococcus aureus disease, and high inflammatory markers.
Persistent fever, disproportionate pain, and non-falling C-reactive protein levels after drainage suggest adjacent osteomyelitis.
MRI of the entire anatomic segment is recommended to detect metaphyseal abscesses or cortical breaches requiring additional drainage.
reference_quote: Empiric antibiotics for septic arthritis should cover Staphylococcus aureus and age-appropriate organisms immediately after aspiration. (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Assessment and management of adjacent osteomyelitis
Clinical and imaging markers for treatment adjustment
